California
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornmission
August 9′  ■990
」ohn G. A■■en
Director
Ramona Municipal Water District
■66■■ Highway Valley Road
Ramona′  CA  92065
Re:  Your Request for lnfo■
■ua■ Ass■stance
Our Fi■e No. I-90-384
Dear Mr. ailert
This is in response to your letter requesting assistance
concerning your respons ibi l ities as a director of the Ramona
Munj.cipal water District pursuant to the conflict-of- interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the nasgrr1.1/ Si-nce your
letter requests general guidance, we are treating your request as
one for informal assistance and have provided the following
general guidelines.2 /
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken pIace. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. 
The
Cornrnission does not act as the finder of fact. 
(fn re ooelsbv
(1,975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, copy enclosed. )
OUESTTONS
1. what are your obligations under the Act with respect to
decisions that nay affect your son's plumbing business?
2. where you are disqualified with respect to certain por-
tions of a capital improvenent plan which affect your reaf
property, may you participate in other aspects of the sane plan?
L/ covernment code sections 81ooo-91015. Al-1 statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
section 18000, et seq. A1l, references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 5 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/ Informal assj.stance does not provide the requestor with the
imrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114,' Regulation 18329 (c) (3).)
428J Street, Suite8OO . P.O. BoxSO? . Sacrarnento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (gt6)322-566}


Our Fi■e No.  I-90-384
Page 2
coNcLUsroNs
1. You may not participate in any decision in which your son
or his business is directly before you as an applicant or the
subject of the decision. You must also disqualify as to any deci-
sion which will indirectly naterially affect your son or his busi-
ness .
2. 
You may not participate in any decision which will
foreseeably and naterially affect your economic interests, or will
affect the outcone of a decision from which you were disqualified.
Thus, where other portions of the capital inprovement plan cone
before the district 
which will not affect those portions of the
plan for which you were disqualified, and in which you have no
econornic. interest, you may participate.
FACTS
You are a director for the Ranona Municipal water District
(the ldistrict") 
. You a]so own property served by the district.
Recentty the district 
began implenenting a project to increase
water service to the district. 
You stated as part of that plan,
the district 
had purchased a portion of your property for a water
storage tank. You abstained from the district's 
decj.sions
concerning the acquisition of your land.
Now that the district 
has begun construction of the tank, you
have become concerned that you may not be able to participate in
other decisions concerning the ptan. You stated that the district
is norking with approxinately eight to twelve land owners on
aesthetics requirernents for pipelines to feed the tank. In addi-
tion, the district 
is realigrningT and resurfacing a najor road
which services the area.
In addition, you anticipate that the district will be
considering proposals by developers to acquire water hook ups for
new developnents. you stated you are concerned because your son's
business wil,1 be bidding on plurnbing contracts put out to bid by
nany of the developers. Your son's business is a corporation to
which you loaned money for start up costs. In addition, you act
as a salesnan for your son's business, however, you stated you do
not own any of the stock or receive any conpensation for your
services .
ANALYSI S
The Political Reforrn Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the
confl ict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act rras to ensure that
public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would perform
their duties in an inpartial nanner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported then. (Section 81001(b).)


Our Fi■e No.
Page 3
I-90-384
In furtherance of this goal, section 871,00 of the Act
provi.des :
No public official 
at any leve1 of state or
1oca1 government shal1 make, participate in naking
or in any way attenpt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to kno!, he has a financial
interest.
As a director of the Ramona Municipal water District, 
you are
a 'rpublic official" 
as defined in the Act. 
(Section 82048.)
Thus, you may not use your official 
position to influence a
governmental decision in which you know or have reason to know you
trave a financial interest.
Participation in governmental decisions has been interpreted
broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act. Participation
inctudes votinq, rnaking an appointnent, connitting an agency to a
course of action, entering into a contractual agreement on behalf
of the aftency, deternining not to act, negotiating, advising or
naking recommendations to the decision-maker. In addition, where
a public official 
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise at-
tenpts to inf l-uence, any member, off j,cer, employee or consultant
of the official's 
agency concerning a governmental decision, the
official 
is considered to have used his official position to
influence the decision.3/ 
(Regulations 1870o and 18700.1, copies
enclosed. )
Econonic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a naterial financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public Aenerql1y, on the
official 
or a mernber of his or her inmediate fanilya/ or on:
(b) Any real property in which the public of-
ficial has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dolLars ($1,000) or nore.
1,rt 
Clearly this is true even srhere the ultinate decision is nade
by another governmental body or the voters themselves. (Scher
Advice Letter, No. A-88-479, copy enclosed.)
4/ Section 82029 defines rrinmed.iate familyr to include an
official,s 
spouse and dependent children. Thus, an adult son is
not a menber of the officiaLrs imrnediate fanily for purposes of
the-Act.


our File No.
Page 4
I-90-384
(c) Any source of inco:ne, other than gifts
and other than Loans by a cornmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public lrithout regard to
official 
status, aggregating trrro hundred fifty dot-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or pronised to the pubtic official 
within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of managenent.
Section 87103 (b) , (c) and (d) .
Accgrding to section 87103, as an enployee of your son's
business, even an unsalaried employee, your son's business is an
economic interest. 
Further, lrhere an official 
Dakes a loan to
another person, the person becomes a source of promised incorne for
the fuII anount of the loan until the loan is repaid. 
(Section
87103(c); Gross Advice Letter, No. A-84-040, copy enclosed.)
Thus, your son's business is a source of income to you for the
balancL of the money you loaned to the business, and a potentially
disqualifying economic interest.
In addition, in the Hentschke Advice Letter (No. A-80-069,
copy enclosed) , we said:
In keeping with the purposes of the Act we
conclude that in this case the president/najority
shareholder of the corporation for which DIr. Larson
works rnay also be considered a source of incorne to
Mr. Larson. Although for other purposes the
corporation would be considered Mr. Larson's source
of income, there can be no question that in a
closely-held corporation situation suclr as here the
president/rnaj ority shareholder of a corporation
effectively controls the enployrnent relationship
itself. 
Accordingty we conclude that the uaj ority
sharehoLder is a source of income to l,!r. Larson
and he should therefore disqualify hirnself from any
decision which would have a material financial ef-
fect on the shareholder.
Since it appears from your letter that your son orvns his
plunbinq business as a sole proprietor, both your son and his
business are considered sources of income to you. Consequently,
you uray not participate in any governmental decision if you know
or have reason to know the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on your son or his business.
Foreseeabi l itv
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the tirne a governmental decision is nade


Our Fi■e NO.
Page 5
エー90-384
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. certaintv is not recruired.
Iloerever, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed. )
The general rule set forth in In re Thorner (suDra) is that
where the business entity in which the official 
has a econonic
interest makes a bid on a contract or is preparing to nake a bid,
a fi.nancial effect on the business entity is reasonably
foreseeable even if there is substantial competition. (In re
gh.gLDCL supra. )
For exanple, in lhgrnC-r the actual decision before the public
official 
concerned requests for water variances for property
slated for development. There, the Commission deternined that
such decisions would foreseeablv affect business entities who had
bid or were oreparinq to bid on a contract lrith the developers or
$rho were likeIy to supply the developers with goods.
consequently, when your son's busj,ness has bid or is planning to
bid on a plunbing contract with a developer, district 
decisions on
the developrnent will foreseeably affect your son's business.
Materi a l itv
Regulation 1a7o2.1 (copy enclosed) provides that the effect
of a decision is material if any business entity in which a public
official 
has an econornic interest is directlv involved in the
decision before the public official's 
agency. Your son's business
i,s directly involved in a decision before the board of supervisors
when the business, or the business' agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in lrhich the
decision will be rnade by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or sirnilar reguest or;
(2) Is a naned party in, or the subject of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official 
or the official,s 
agency;
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlenent to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.
Regulation 18702.1(b) .
where your sonrs business is directlv involved in a decision
before the water district 
you are r"q"GE-E-Gquaiity 
yoursert
fron participating in the board decision,


Our Fi■e No.  I-90-384
Page 6
In addition, the Act requires an official to disguatify hin
or herself from participation in governmental decisions which
indirectlv have a materi.al financial, effect on a business entity
in which he or she has a financial interest. whether the indirect
effect on a business is material depends on the financial size of
the business entity. Regulation 1a7O2.2 (copy enclosed) provides
differing standards of materiality which apply lrhere a business
entity is listed on the New York stock Exchange or American Stock
Exchange or Fortune lIagazine Directory of the 5OO largest U.S.
Industrial corporations (Reg'ulation 18702.2(a) and (d)); or where
the business entity is listed on the National Association of
Securities Dealers National !{arket List (Regulation 18702.2(b),
(e) and (f)),' or where the busj.ness entity is listed on the
Pacific Stock Exchange (Regnrlation 18702.2(c)).
Regulation L8702.2(g) provides that for a relatively smaIl
business entity, the indirect effect of a decision is naterial
where:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of 910,000 or nore; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or elininating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or nore; or
(3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or Liabilities
of $10,000 or nore.
In the alternative, if a district decision naterially affects
your son personally, you are also required to disqualify with
respect to the decision. Regulation LA7OZ.6 provides:
The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official if any of the following applies:
(a) The decision will affect the
individual.,s income, investnents, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $f,OOO or morei
or


our File No.
Paqe 7
■-90-384
indi
ner
■870
) The decision wil■
 affect the
ua■′s rea■ property interest in a man―
t is considered mater■
a■ under Section
or Section ■8702.4. ′
consequently, where either source of income is directly
before you as an applicant or the subject of the decision, Regula-
tion 18702.1(a) provides that the effect of the decision on the
source of income is deemed materj.al and disgualification is
required. 
(webb Advice Letter, No. I-89-415, copy enclosed.)
Where neither source of income is directly before you, you Dust
sti11 disqualify yourself srhere the sources of income will be
indirectly naterially affected.
However, Regulation 18700.1 provides that an otherwise
disqualified official 
may appear in the same manner as any other
rnenber of the general public before his or her agency in the
course of its prescrj,bed governnental function to rePresent his or
her personal interests in real property if the property is wholly
owned by the official 
or members of his or her irnnediate farnily.
Thus, if you are the sole owner of your property you may appear
before the Ramona Municipal water District in the sane nanner as
any other member of the public, to advocate on behalf of your
property interests. 
Hovrever, your coruaents nust be linited to
your personal interests and you should take care to clarify that
you are not acting in any official 
capacity. 
(Larsen Advice Let-
ter, No. A-87-151, copy enclosed.) The councilmenbers stiIl 
would
be prohibited from privately discussing these natters with other
members of the city council or with other city officials.
Decisions Concernino the Storaoe Tank
As stated above, your property interests are independent
potentially disgualifying econoruic interests under the Act. 
Your
properties, proxirnity to property which is the subject to a
decj,sions may also result in disqualification. 
For exanple,
Regulation L87O2.3 (copy enclosed) provides that the effect of a
decision is material as to your real property if any of the fol-
).owing applies: (1) Your real property, or any pait of your real
property, is located within a 3OO foot radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject
of the decision, unless the decisi.on wil1 have no financial eifect
upon your real property. (Z) The decision involves construction
of,.or irnprovements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or
sinilar facilities, 
and your real" property will receive new or
substantially improved servj.ces. (3) your real property is
located outside a radius of 3OO feet and any part of- youi real
property is located within a radius of 2,500 ieet of Ltre

cl"/ - Regulations 18702.3 and La7o2.4 have been enclosed for your
infornation.


our File No.
Page 8
I-90-384
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property nhich is
the subject of the decision and the decision will have a reason-
ably foreseeable financial effect of: 
(A) $10,0oo or more on the
fair market value of your real property; or (B) will affect the
rental value of your property by $1,oo0 or uore per 12 month
period.
In our advice Letter to llillian 
Sabourin (Advice Letter No.
A-89-708, copy enclosed) you were advised that you could not
participate in decisions concerning a rrater tank to be located on
your property or in decisions concerning an easenent across your
property. The letter stated:
As I indicated on the telephone, lilr. Allen nay
participate in any district 
decision concerning the
caoital imorovement Dlan that does not relate to or
have an effect upon the value of his real propertv.
As part of this, I indicated that ur. Allen could
not, therefore, participate in district 
decisions
concerningr the district's 
acquisition of his
property or the district's 
placement of a water
reservoir tank upon the property. 
(See Sections
87100, and 87103(b), and Regulation 78702.1, copy
enclosed. )
Final1y, you inforrned ne that placement of the
water reservoir tank on property acquired from IrIr.
Allen nay involve placing underground pipes on
other property owned by !fr. Allen that will not be
acquired by the district. 
you asked if I,!r. Allen
could participate in district decisions concernj.ng
this natter. You also indicated that you did not
think the placenent of these pipes would have any
financial inpact upon Ur. A11en,s property.
By installation of these pipes, it appears
that the district is creating an easernent for
itself on Mr. A11en,s property. Regulation
La102.1(a) (3) (A) (copy enclosed) generally
prohibits an official fron participating in deci-
sions concerning the sa1e, lease or rta similar
decision" involving his or her property. Since an
easement confers a right on its holder to some
profit, benefit, doninion or lawful use out of or
over the land of another (Guerra v. packard (1955)
236 Cal.App.2d 272), it is sinilar in many respects
to a sale or lease of real property. Thua, Regula-
tion 18702.1 would apply to the pllcenent of these
pi.pes across Mr. A1len,s property. Subdivision
(c) (2) of Regulation tB7O2.1 pernits Ur. Al1en to


Our Fi■e No.
Page 9
■-90-384
participate in decisions concerning the easement
if, in fact, the easement does not have a financial
effect on Mr. A11en's property.
You have also asked if you may now participate in other deci-
si,ons of the district. 
cenerally, decisions are analyzed
independently to deterrnine if there will be a foreseeable material
financial effect on an official's 
financial interest. 
(In re owen
(1976) 2 FPPC ops. 77, copy enclosed.) Consequently, many tirnes,
Iarge and complex decisions may be divided i,nto separate decisions
so that where a public official 
has a disqualifying financial
interest as to one component of the decision, he Day still
participate i.n considering the other components provided the deci-
sions are not interrelated and the official 
has no disqualifylng
interest lvith respect to the other components. (Huffaker Advice
Letter, No. A-86-343, copy enclosed.)
Horrever, under sone circurnstances a series of decisions rnay
be too interrelated to be considered separately. (MiIIer Advice
Letter, No. A-82-119, copy enclosed.) For exanple, since you were
disqualified with respect to the easenent that lri11 run across your
property, you may not participate in other decisions concerning
other portions of the easement which would result in the entire
project being compJ.eted or aborted. Further, since you were
disqualified as to decisions concerning the storage tank, you are
also required to disqualify as all other decisions related to the
storage tank if these decisions will either affect the fair narket
value of your property or affect the conpletion of the storage tank
plan from which you were disqualified.
Since you have not provided sufficient inforrnation with
respect to these other decisions for us to deterrnine whether they
are severable fron the decisions for which you were disqualified,
we can only provide you with these very general guidelines. 
You
should contact the water districtrs 
attorney with respect to
factual questions concerning the application of these ru1es.
f trust this letter has addressed your concerns. should you
have any further guestions regarding this natter or a specific
decision that you would like advice on, please feel free to contact
ne at (915) 322-59oL.
S incerely,
Scott HalLabrin
Acting ceneral CounseL
